Wait - this doesn't make sense. If we turned RIGHT and the bearing went from -106 to -118 (more negative), that means the goal is now even more to our right/behind. But turning right should have brought us closer to the goal if it was on our right...

Unless... the bearing sign convention is opposite to what I thought. If negative means the goal is to our LEFT, then:
- -106 degrees means goal is 106 degrees to our left (behind_left)
- -118 degrees means goal is 118 degrees to our left (even more behind_left)

So turning RIGHT made us turn

text:
The TURN_RIGHT was actually good! The reward is nearly neutral (-0.010) and the distance stayed the same (1918 cm), which means we stopped regressing. The bearing changed from -118 to -148 degrees, and our yaw changed from -120 to -90 degrees, confirming we turned right.

The bearing is now -148 degrees, which is "behind_right" territory (more than 90 degrees to the right/behind). According to the experience:
- "When behind_right & far: TURN_LEFT → neutral (avg +0cm, seen 810x). Turned away from goal (bearing was behind_right). Turn the other way."

This means when the bearing is behind_right, we should turn RIGHT (not left) to face the goal. We just did that and it worked - we stopped regressing.

Now we need to continue turning right to get more aligned with the goal. The bearing is -148 degrees, which is still quite far from aligned (0 degrees). We need to turn right again to reduce this bearing angle.

Action: TURN_RIGHT
